Prioritizing tree species conservation in a megadiverse South
American region: integrating taxonomic and phylogenetic
metrics

Abstract
Plant biodiversity is under increasing threat due to habitat loss, land-use change, and
climate pressures, particularly in megadiverse regions such as the La Plata Basin (LPB).
Here, we present a spatially explicit analysis integrating taxonomic and phylogenetic diver-
sity metrics to identify biodiversity hotspots and conservation gaps for tree species across
the LPB. Using occurrence data for tree species and metrics including species richness
(SR), phylogenetic diversity (PD), standardized effect size of PD (ses.PD), evolutionary

ross
the LPB. Using occurrence data for tree species and metrics including species richness
(SR), phylogenetic diversity (PD), standardized effect size of PD (ses.PD), evolutionary
distinctiveness (ED), and biogeographically weighted ED (BED), we mapped patterns of
evolutionary value and evaluated their alignment with existing protected areas. Our results
reveal marked geographic disparities among the metrics, with eastern LPB regions highly
impacted by agriculture and urbanization still retaining significant evolutionary heritage.
Additionally, the Andes, Cerrado, and Pantanal also emerged as key areas for phylogenetic
conservation. However, current protected areas fail to adequately represent these hotspots,
highlighting urgent conservation gaps. We emphasize the need for targeted spatial strate-
gies that integrate evolutionary history and land-use planning to enhance the effectiveness
of conservation networks. This approach provides a comprehensive framework to guide
biodiversity protection in tropical landscapes under rapid anthropogenic change.

Introduction

Plant biodiversity, particularly in tropical regions, is undergoing rapid and unprecedented
decline due to a combination of land-use change, habitat fragmentation, and climate change

ant biodiversity, particularly in tropical regions, is undergoing rapid and unprecedented
decline due to a combination of land-use change, habitat fragmentation, and climate change
(Butchart et al. 2010; Barnosky et al. 2011; Deikumah et al. 2014; Potapov et al. 2017; Tau-
bert et al. 2018; Joly et al. 2019; Habel et al. 2019; D√≠az et al. 2019; FAO and UNEP 2020;
Fischer et al. 2021). The ongoing conversion of natural ecosystems to agricultural, urban,
and industrial landscapes poses severe threats to global biodiversity (Barnosky et al. 2011;
Joly et al. 2019; D√≠az et al. 2019), particularly in regions of high endemism and evolution-
ary uniqueness. It is estimated that nearly 40% of vascular plant species are currently at risk
of extinction, a figure that far exceeds natural background rates (Lughadha et al. 2020). In
response, international frameworks such as the Aichi Biodiversity Targets and the post-2020
Global Biodiversity Framework have emphasized the need to protect not only species but
also the ecological and evolutionary processes that sustain biodiversity (P√∂rtner et al. 2021).
    However, progress toward these goals remains uneven. While protected areas (PAs) have

he ecological and evolutionary processes that sustain biodiversity (P√∂rtner et al. 2021).
    However, progress toward these goals remains uneven. While protected areas (PAs) have
expanded globally, many fail to encompass key regions of biological and evolutionary sig-
nificance (Watson et al. 2014; Visconti et al. 2019). Protected area networks often prioritize
areas of political or economic convenience over ecological necessity, leading to systematic
gaps in conservation coverage (Joppa and Pfaff 2009; Watson et al. 2014). Moreover, tra-
ditional conservation planning has relied heavily on taxonomic measures such as species
richness (SR). Species richness alone can obscure important biodiversity patterns, as it fails
to capture the full complexity of ecosystems (Tallents et al. 2005), and may be unable to
clarify for the evolutionary diversity within communities (Forest et al. 2007; Li et al. 2015;
Qian and Deng 2021).
    To address these limitations and better capture the evolutionary dimension of biodiver-
sity in conservation strategies, there has been growing recognition of the need to integrate

To address these limitations and better capture the evolutionary dimension of biodiver-
sity in conservation strategies, there has been growing recognition of the need to integrate
phylogenetic diversity into conservation planning (Cadotte and Davies 2010; Tucker et
al. 2019). Phylogenetic diversity (PD), often calculated as the sum of branch lengths in a
phylogenetic tree spanning a set of species (Faith‚Äôs PD), represents a crucial dimension of
biodiversity by capturing feature diversity and option values for conservation (Faith 1992;
Carstensen et al. 2013). Increasingly recognized as an essential component of global biodi-
versity (Pollock et al. 2015, 2017), PD has been linked to enhanced ecosystem productivity
(Cadotte 2013; Coelho De Souza et al. 2019) and human well-being.
    Several complementary metrics have been developed to capture distinct dimensions of
phylogenetic diversity trees (Redding 2003; Pavoine et al. 2005, 2017; Redding and Moo-
ers 2006; Isaac et al. 2007; Redding et al. 2008). Among them, evolutionary distinctiveness
(ED) quantifies how evolutionarily isolated a species is on a phylogenetic tree, capturing

s 2006; Isaac et al. 2007; Redding et al. 2008). Among them, evolutionary distinctiveness
(ED) quantifies how evolutionarily isolated a species is on a phylogenetic tree, capturing
rare or unique features that may contribute significantly to ecosystem functions (Redding
et al. 2010). These species are often irreplaceable and of high conservation priority due to
their limited genetic redundancy and contribution to ecosystem resilience (Morelli 2018).
To capture the full scope of biodiversity, methods integrating evolutionary history and geo-
graphical rarity have been developed (Rosauer et al. 2009; Cadotte and Davies 2010). The

    By considering the evolutionary history embedded within ecosystems, this approach not
only preserves species diversity but also safeguards the unique genetic and functional traits
that have evolved over time (Forest et al. 2007; Safi et al. 2011; Pavoine et al. 2013; Zupan
et al. 2014; Schweizer et al. 2014; Tucker et al. 2019; Molina-Venegas et al. 2021; Molina-
Venegas 2021; Ge et al. 2022; Cardillo 2023). The conservation of phylogenetic diversity
(PD) is crucial because extinction is not random within the tree of life; closely related spe-
cies often face similar levels of threat (Purvis et al. 2000; Safi et al. 2011). Maintaining PD
helps ensure ecosystem resilience and adaptability to environmental changes, providing a
comprehensive framework for effective biodiversity conservation.
    Incorporating phylogenetic information into spatial planning can substantially improve
the effectiveness of conservation networks by highlighting areas of irreplaceable evolu-
tionary value that are often overlooked by traditional taxonomic approaches (Pollock et

ove
the effectiveness of conservation networks by highlighting areas of irreplaceable evolu-
tionary value that are often overlooked by traditional taxonomic approaches (Pollock et
al. 2017; Mouillot et al. 2013; Molina-Venegas et al. 2021). This is especially relevant in
heterogeneous and megadiverse landscapes, where complex biogeographic histories and
environmental gradients have shaped diverse evolutionary trajectories.
    The La Plata Basin (LPB), one of the most megadiverse regions in South America, exem-
plifies such complexity. Spanning five countries, Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and
Uruguay, it encompasses a mosaic of ecosystems including tropical forests, wetlands, savan-
nas, and montane habitats. This region harbors a high number of endemic and threatened
plant species and plays a crucial role in the provision of ecosystem services and the mainte-
nance of evolutionary heritage (Clavijo et al. 2024). The basin is densely populated by more
than 110 million people, including S√£o Paulo (Brazil), the largest city in South America,
as well as large cities such as Buenos Aires, Bras√≠lia, Asunci√≥n and Montevideo (WWAP

e
than 110 million people, including S√£o Paulo (Brazil), the largest city in South America,
as well as large cities such as Buenos Aires, Bras√≠lia, Asunci√≥n and Montevideo (WWAP
- UNESCO 2007). However, rapid agricultural expansion, infrastructure development, and
the construction of large hydroelectric reservoirs have intensified land-use change in the
region (Zarfl et al. 2015; Schlindwein et al. 2021), placing mounting pressure on native
vegetation and evolutionary lineages. Despite its ecological and evolutionary importance,
the LPB remains underrepresented in global conservation efforts. Many of its most criti-
cal areas for biodiversity conservation are still poorly studied and inadequately protected.
This highlights the urgent need for spatially explicit approaches that integrate phylogenetic
diversity into conservation planning to better inform policy and guide resource allocation.
    Assessments of biodiversity hotspots (areas of high diversity and/or endemism) and gap
analyses are essential to increase the efficiency of the nature reserve network and provide
information to improve its management. To this end, we applied spatial analyses to analy-

nalyses are essential to increase the efficiency of the nature reserve network and provide
information to improve its management. To this end, we applied spatial analyses to analy-
ses to elucidate biodiversity distribution patterns and identify conservation hotspots, using
distribution data for tree species within the LPB. By employing well-established metrics
such as phylogenetic diversity (PD), standardized effect size of PD (ses.PD), evolutionary
distinctiveness (ED), and biogeographically weighted evolutionary distinctiveness (BED),
we aim to capture the evolutionary dimensions of biodiversity. Additionally, we identi-
fied species categorized as threatened according to the IUCN Red List. These biodiversity
hotspots were subsequently compared with the network of existing protected areas (PAs) to
detect potential conservation gaps, guiding where conservation efforts should be prioritized.

Such assessments are vital in predicting species extinction risks, identifying key knowledge
gaps, and evaluating the efficacy of PAs in preserving biodiversity. By focusing on whether
centers of endemism and evolutionary significance align with protected areas, we can bet-
ter understand the conservation status of species with restricted distributions, particularly
endemics, which are often more susceptible to environmental changes. Thus, we aimed (1)
to map the primary hotspots of plant diversity within the La Plata Basin; (2) to assess the
current effectiveness of the PA network; and (3) to identify and address conservation gaps,
ensuring the long-term preservation of the basin‚Äôs unique and threatened biodiversity.


Material and methods

Study area

La Plata Basin, located in the south-central portion of South America, is the fifth-largest
basin in the world and the second in the continent covering 3,286,875 km2. This vast area
encompasses five countries, Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay‚ÄĒwith over

basin in the world and the second in the continent covering 3,286,875 km2. This vast area
encompasses five countries, Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay‚ÄĒwith over
100 million inhabitants (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1). This basin includes tropical and subtropical
climates (ranging 14¬į 30‚Ä≤ S to 36¬į 30‚Ä≤ S latitude) from sea level up to 3,600 m.a.m.s.l. and
some of the largest rivers in the world such as Paran√°, Paraguay, and Uruguay. The basin
also includes the largest wetlands in South America (Pantanal and Iber√°), one of the most
productive grasslands in the world (Pampa), a semi-arid region (Chaco), rainforests (Atlan-
tic and Paranaense Forest) as well as mountain regions rich in minerals and the second-
world-largest aquifer (Guarani aquifer) (CIC - Intergovernmental Coordinating Committee
of the Countries of Cuenca del Plata 2017).

    The LPB region is one of the richest in terms of biodiversity and ecosystem variety in the
world, comparable to the Amazon River system m (Organization of American States (OAS)
(2005); Flinker 2012; CIC - Intergovernmental Coordinating Committee of the Countries
of Cuenca del Plata 2017), due to its geographic location and the diversity of ecoregions,
ranging from arid and semi-arid areas to humid forests (Pontes et al. 2015), Seasonally Dry
Tropical Forests (Atlantic Forest of Alto-Paran√°, Chiquitano Dry Forest, Montane Dry For-
est of Bolivia), Savannas (Cerrado), Chaco (Humid and Dry Chaco), Pantanal, and Pampa
(Olson et al. 2001). In most of the basin, annual average temperatures exceed 20 ¬įC, rang-
ing from approximately 15 ¬įC in the South to more than 25 ¬įC in the Northwest (Barros et
al. 2006; WWAP ‚Äď UNESCO 2007; Sola 2014; Food and Agriculture Organization of the

al. 2006; WWAP ‚Äď UNESCO 2007; Sola 2014; Food and Agriculture Organization of the
United Nations (FAO), 2016). The LPB experiences a wide range of climates, from the
hot and dry Chaco region to the humid southern areas of Brazil and southeastern Paraguay
(CIC - Intergovernmental Coordinating Committee of the Countries of Cuenca del Plata
2017). The basin‚Äôs average annual precipitation is around 1,100 mm, but there are signifi-
cant regional differences (Mechoso et al. 2001). In the northern parts of the basin, rainfall
can surpass 1,500 mm per year, whereas in the southernmost areas, it drops to below 900
mm annually (Cer√≥n et al. 2021). This climatic heterogeneity contributes to the region‚Äôs
environmental complexity and rich biodiversity.


Database

Floristic data were obtained from the NeoTropTree database (Oliveira-Filho 2017), a com-
pilation of tree species Lists and occurrence records collected from the Literature and her-
barium specimens. The final dataset included 146,395 occurrence records and 3,102 tree

pilation of tree species Lists and occurrence records collected from the Literature and her-
barium specimens. The final dataset included 146,395 occurrence records and 3,102 tree
species (Fig. 1). We divided the entire extent of the La Plata Basin into regular grid cells of
50 √ó 50 km and overlaid the NeoTropTree assemblages onto this grid. Only cells contain-
ing at least one floristic checklist were retained; empty cells were excluded due to the lack
of data for biodiversity metrics. For each grid cell, we aggregated all species records from
the checklists falling within its boundaries. Thus, each cell represents the combined species
pool from one or more assemblages, which was then used to compute species richness,
endemism, and phylogenetic metrics. As found by Cantidio and Souza (2019), abundance
data can provide more ecological information than presence‚Äďabsence data. However, large-
scale abundance datasets covering the entire region are not publicly available (but see De
Lima et al. 2015 for one of the most comprehensive efforts to date). Ferns and gymnosperms
were excluded from our analyses due to their rarity in most parts of the Atlantic Forest and

Phylogenetic diversity analysis

We generated a phylogenetic tree including a pooling of all species using a mega-tree
approach in the R package V.PhyloMaker2 (Jin and Qian 2022). This mega-tree contains
74,533 vascular plant species, based on the APG IV phylogenetic system of angiosperm
classification, and includes all plant families (Jin and Qian 2022). We opted to use the sce-

    To examine the distribution of diversity of plants in the LPB we calculated for each
grid cell measures of (i) species richness, (SR), used as a measure of taxonomic diversity,
representing the total number of species present within each grid; (ii) Faith‚Äôs phylogenetic
diversity (PD) (Faith 1992), which is the total sum of branch lengths in a phylogeny that
includes species from the community and measures total evolutionary history; (iii) ses.PD, a
standardized version of PD accounting for species richness (Webb 2000; Webb et al. 2002).
The standardization process involved randomly selecting the same number of species from
the phylogeny as present in the community, with 999 repetitions. The difference between
the observed PD and the mean PD of the randomizations was then divided by the standard
deviation of the randomized PD values to generate a standardized effect size. To describe the
singularity of the species, we extracted the scores of (iv) evolutionary distinctiveness (ED)

on of the randomized PD values to generate a standardized effect size. To describe the
singularity of the species, we extracted the scores of (iv) evolutionary distinctiveness (ED)
from the phylogenies, following Isaac et al. 2007. Species with small range sizes compared
with those that are widely distributed are probably much more vulnerable because their
habitats may vanish under global climate change (Cadotte and Davies 2010). To calculate
the BED of a species, one can partition phylogenetic branch lengths by descendant species‚Äô
abundances or by the number of populations or in our case, the number of occupied grid
cells. The metric BED is then the summation of BED values of all species in a site; thus,
sites with species that are narrowly distributed will have higher BED than sites with widely
distributed species (Tucker et al. 2012).

of species. In the first stage, we included all grid cells, totaling 1181 grids, regardless of
the presence of species. This approach allowed a comprehensive assessment of diversity,

Identification of biodiversity hotspots

Maps were compared using seven metrics to identify areas with high diversity (SR, PD,
ses.PD, average ED, ED proportion >5; ED proportion < 5 and BED). Any 50 km √ó 50 km
grid cell was marked as a hotspot if its value for a given index was within the top 10% of
its range. To visualize areas with multimetrics data, we Generated overlap maps of the top
10% hotspots. We then assessed whether these biodiversity hotspots were located within

   Protected areas in the LPB were obtained from the World Database on Protected Areas
(WDPA), the most comprehensive global database of terrestrial and marine protected areas
(Fig S2). This study exclusively considers Strictly Protected Conservation Units, whose pri-
mary objective is nature preservation, allowing only indirect use of their natural resources
(Matarazzo and Serva 2021). Sustainable Use Units were excluded, as these areas balance
environmental conservation with the sustainable use of some natural resources (Brazil -
National System of Nature Conservation Units 2000), providing a broader management
approach. Additionally, the existing impacts in these areas represent an additional factor for
their exclusion (Fran√ßoso et al. 2015; Heringer et al. 2020).
   For each index, this study follows a widely used method for identifying conservation
priority areas, defining them as the top 5% of grid cells (Daru et al. 2016; Elliott et al. 2018;
Xue et al. 2021). Equal weights were assigned to each layer to determine conservation pri-

areas, defining them as the top 5% of grid cells (Daru et al. 2016; Elliott et al. 2018;
Xue et al. 2021). Equal weights were assigned to each layer to determine conservation pri-
ority areas, and the conservation network was subsequently overlaid to assess conservation
effectiveness (Zhang et al. 2024).


Results

We assembled a phylogeny of tree species in the LPB, comprising 3,102 species across 644
Genera and 119 families. Six families account for 44% of the species: Fabaceae (412 spp.),
Myrtaceae (369 spp.), Rubiaceae (167 spp.), Melastomataceae (165 spp.), Lauraceae (135
spp.), and Asteraceae (111 spp.). The most diverse genera were Eugenia (128 spp.), Myrcia
(93 spp.), Miconia (92 spp.), Ocotea (67 spp.), and Solanum (60 spp.). Overall, our results
revealed that the diversity and endemism of tree species showed uneven distribution in LPB.


Spatial patterns of diversity metrics

Overall, there were differences in the geographic locations of the hotspots identified by
each index (Fig. 2). As expected, phylogenetic diversity was highly correlated with species
richness (Fig. S3). Specifically, phylogenetic and taxonomic diversity were high in the east

   Considering that species richness (SR) is positively correlated with phylogenetic diver-

Diversity hotspots and conservation status

According to the results for the 5% hotspots, 59 grids were identified (Fig. 3g) as hotspots
for at least one diversity index, totaling 147,500 km2 (Fig. 3). No overlap was found among
the hotspots identified for the six evaluated metrics, indicating that these metrics capture
different aspects of biodiversity. We identified 25 overlapping grids among four metrics,
located mainly in the eastern region of the La Plata Basin, as well as some grids in the

e
different aspects of biodiversity. We identified 25 overlapping grids among four metrics,
located mainly in the eastern region of the La Plata Basin, as well as some grids in the
northeastern and northern regions of the LPB. Areas with three or two diversity hotspots
exhibited a more dispersed pattern.
    Therefore, most of the hotspots identified in our analyses within the LPB are not well
protected. The protection level in the La Plata Basin has been uneven, with varying cover-
age degrees across different diversity metrics. It was observed that only 44.06% of the SR
and PD hotspots are located within protected areas, while the majority (55.93%) remain
unprotected. This lack of protection is even more pronounced for ED (evolutionary distinc-
tiveness), with only 20.33% of hotspots protected, and ses.PD (standardized phylogenetic
diversity), with a mere 15.25% under protection. BED hotspots demonstrated slightly better
protection, with 45.76% located in protected areas; however, over half of these hotspots are
still outside conservation zones.

    Regarding the overlap of the proportion of top 5% and proportion of bottom 5% ED
values, only 35.59% and 44.06%, respectively, are protected, highlighting significant con-
servation gaps. Conservation gaps were also observed for hotspots identified by four and
three diversity metrics. The intersections between hotspots identified by all four metrics
are particularly critical for biodiversity conservation because the highest index values were
found in these areas. Alarmingly, we observed that 53.48% of the intersections between
three and four metrics occur outside protected areas. When comparing protected areas under
the IUCN and MMA systems, the degree of protection of the MMA is notably higher. Pro-
tected areas classified under the IUCN system cover 66.53% of the hotspots, while those
under the MMA system 68.87%, demonstrating greater effectiveness than other metrics
(Fig. 4). However, this level of protection remains insufficient to encompass all biodiversity
hotspots.

Discussion

Our study proposed an approach based on phylogenetic diversity metrics to identify bio-
diversity hotspots and guide the prioritization of conservation areas in the La Plata Basin

As one of the world‚Äôs most prominent biodiversity hotspots, the Atlantic Forest is shaped
by a complex evolutionary and environmental history influenced by tectonic activity, paleo-
geographic reconfigurations, and climatic fluctuations since the Neogene (Rull 2011). These
historical processes promoted the formation of forest refugia, enabling species persistence
and diversification (Carnaval et al. 2009; Batalha-Filho et al. 2019). Regions such as Serra
do Mar and Serra da Mantiqueira, characterized by sharp altitudinal gradients and high
environmental heterogeneity, offer favorable conditions for species establishment and niche
expansion. This heterogeneity facilitates species coexistence and diversification via vicari-
ance, reinforcing the high levels of richness, endemism, and phylogenetic diversity recorded
in the region (Stein and Kreft 2015; Ferreira et al. 2024).

Despite the ecological and evolutionary importance of these areas, our findings reveal
that the current protected area network in the La Plata Basin performs poorly in safeguard-
ing species richness, phylogenetic diversity, and threatened taxa. Many of the most relevant

critical conservation gaps. This reinforces the urgent need for more targeted and systematic
conservation strategies focused on evolutionary metrics. Integrating multiple biodiversity
dimensions can help guide the expansion of protected areas toward regions of irreplaceable
evolutionary value. Furthermore, conservation planning should prioritize areas where mul-
tiple metrics converge, as these represent core conservation opportunities. Strategies may
include the expansion of strictly protected reserves, the creation of ecological corridors, and
the implementation of adaptive management approaches that balance biodiversity preserva-

    Our study contributes valuable insights into the spatial distribution of evolutionary and
taxonomic diversity across the La Plata Basin and highlights priority regions for conserva-
tion action. In doing so, it also brings attention to a critical issue in large-scale biodiversity
assessments: the uneven spatial distribution of floristic data. Some remote or less accessible
regions remain under-sampled, which may lead to localized underestimations of species

Conclusion

Protected areas play a fundamental role in safeguarding rare and threatened species while
maximizing the protection of ecological and evolutionary processes. In this study, we applied
a phylogenetically informed approach to identify biodiversity hotspots in the La Plata Basin
(LPB), integrating multiple metrics to capture complementary dimensions of diversity. Our
results demonstrate that hotspots differ substantially depending on the metric used, high-
lighting the need for a multi-dimensional approach to conservation planning. In particular,
we show that regions with high evolutionary distinctiveness, phylogenetic diversity, and
high overlap of metrics should be prioritized, as they represent irreplaceable components of
biodiversity. The current protected area network in the LPB does not effectively encompass
these key regions, emphasizing the importance of expanding and optimizing conservation
efforts. Given the rapid pace of land conversion, strategies must integrate land-use planning
with biodiversity conservation, ensuring that protected areas are strategically located and

ts. Given the rapid pace of land conversion, strategies must integrate land-use planning
with biodiversity conservation, ensuring that protected areas are strategically located and
ecologically effective. By incorporating evolutionary history and diversification processes,
our findings provide a solid foundation for a more comprehensive and resilient conservation
agenda in one of South America‚Äôs most diverse and threatened landscapes.
